{{Short description="Law relating the distribution of electric charge to the resulting electric field"}}
{{Infobox physical law
| name = Gauss's law
| image = Karl Friedrich Gauss.jpg
| caption = [[Carl Friedrich Gauss]], after whom the law is named
| formula = <math>\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}</math>
| discovered_by = [[Joseph-Louis Lagrange]] (1773), [[Carl Friedrich Gauss]] (1813)
}}

'''Gauss's law''', also known as '''Gauss's flux theorem''', is a law in [[electrostatics]] relating the distribution of [[electric charge]] to the resulting [[electric field]]. In its integral form, it states that the total [[electric flux]] through any closed surface is proportional to the total electric charge enclosed within that surface:
:<math>\oiint_{\partial V} \mathbf{E} \cdot d\mathbf{A} = \frac{Q_{\text{enc}}}{\varepsilon_0}</math>

In differential form:
:<math>\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}</math>

Gauss's law is one of the four [[Maxwell's equations]] that form the foundation of [[classical electromagnetism]].

== Physical interpretation ==
Electric charges are sources or sinks of the electric field:
* Positive charges are sources (<math>\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} > 0</math>)
* Negative charges are sinks (<math>\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} < 0</math>)
* In charge-free regions, field lines are continuous (<math>\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} = 0</math>)

== Applications ==
Gauss's law is most useful for calculating electric fields when the charge distribution has high symmetry.

=== Spherical symmetry ===
For a point charge or spherically symmetric charge distribution:
:<math>E = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Q_{\text{enc}}}{r^2}</math>
which recovers [[Coulomb's law]].

=== Cylindrical symmetry ===
For an infinite line charge with linear charge density <math>\lambda</math>:
:<math>E = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 r}</math>

=== Planar symmetry ===
For an infinite sheet of charge with surface charge density <math>\sigma</math>:
:<math>E = \frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}</math>

For two oppositely charged parallel plates ([[capacitor]]):
:<math>E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}</math> (between plates)
:<math>E = 0</math> (outside plates)

=== Uniformly charged sphere ===
For a uniformly charged insulating sphere of radius <math>R</math>:
* Outside (<math>r > R</math>): <math>E = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Q}{r^2}</math>
* Inside (<math>r < R</math>): <math>E = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Qr}{R^3}</math>

== Gauss's law in matter ==
In a [[dielectric]] medium, the law takes the form:
:<math>\nabla \cdot \mathbf{D} = \rho_f</math>
where <math>\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon_0\mathbf{E} + \mathbf{P}</math> is the [[electric displacement field]], and <math>\rho_f</math> is the free charge density.

== Equivalence to Coulomb's law ==
Gauss's law and [[Coulomb's law]] are mathematically equivalent for electrostatics. Gauss's law is more fundamental because it holds for time-varying fields as well.

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Gauss's law for magnetism]]
* [[Maxwell's equations]]
* [[Electric flux]]
* [[Divergence theorem]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Gauss's law| ]]
[[Category:Electrostatics]]
[[Category:Maxwell's equations]]
